dimensions.dimension_name: Emotional vs Analytical Ad Processing Style
dimensions.what_it_captures: Whether this person processes advertising primarily through emotional/aesthetic resonance (mood, music, visual storytelling, color palette) or through analytical evaluation (claims scrutiny, ingredient lists, comparative data). The 59 questions capture WHAT content types she prefers (before/after, ingredient breakdowns) but not HOW she cognitively processes the ad stimulus in the first few seconds — whether she's drawn in by emotional tone or informational density.
dimensions.concept_test_impact: An emotionally-driven processor would respond strongly to a concept that leads with a sensory narrative ('imagine the calming embrace of lavender on tender skin') while an analytical processor would respond better to a concept structured as a feature matrix with clinical evidence. This changes which concept framing wins her attention and perceived credibility.
dimensions.ad_test_impact: A video ad with soft music, slow-motion product shots, and minimal text would captivate an emotional processor but frustrate an analytical one who wants to see ingredient callouts and dermatologist endorsements upfront. This directly affects watch-through rates, emotional valence scores, and stated purchase intent.
dimensions.what_we_can_infer: She values ingredient breakdowns (Q57) and ranks ingredients first (Q11), suggesting analytical tendencies. But she also describes herself as 'dramatic' and 'moody' (Q6), and her fragrance attachment is deeply emotional ('flower scents = natural feel-good vibe,' Q37). These signals conflict — we cannot determine her dominant ad processing mode.
dimensions.example_variation: Twin A (emotional processor) sees a 30-second Instagram ad with dreamy lavender fields, soft piano, and a woman smiling post-shower — she feels drawn in, watches fully, and rates purchase intent 8/10. Twin B (analytical processor) sees the same ad and skips at 5 seconds because there were no ingredient callouts or sensitivity claims — rates purchase intent 3/10. Same person profile, opposite reactions.

dimensions.dimension_name: Aspirational Identity vs Pragmatic Self Orientation
dimensions.what_it_captures: Whether she evaluates body wash concepts through the lens of who she aspires to become (elevated lifestyle, self-care ritual, premium identity signaling) or through her current pragmatic reality (functional problem-solving for sensitive skin, value for money, no-fuss routine). The questions capture her current routine and preferences but not whether she's motivated by identity aspiration or practical adequacy when encountering new products.
dimensions.concept_test_impact: A concept positioned as 'elevate your daily ritual into a spa-like self-care moment' would resonate strongly with an aspirational orientation but feel irrelevant to a pragmatic one who just wants 'gentle on sensitive skin, done.' This changes willingness to pay, perceived relevance, and concept appeal scores dramatically.
dimensions.ad_test_impact: An ad featuring a luxurious bathroom setting, premium lifestyle cues, and aspirational messaging ('you deserve this') would attract an aspirational twin but alienate a pragmatic twin who sees it as disconnected from her real life. The pragmatic twin responds better to a straightforward problem-solution ad in a normal bathroom.
dimensions.what_we_can_infer: Her ideal routine includes organic facemasks and juice for skin health (Q10), suggesting some aspirational thinking. But her actual routine is minimal (Q28-Q29), she's mood-dependent about grooming (Q32), and she spends only ₹100-200 (Q21). The gap between aspiration and behavior makes it unclear which lens dominates when she evaluates NEW concepts.
dimensions.example_variation: Both twins see a premium body wash concept at ₹450 positioned as 'artisanal Ayurvedic self-care.' Twin A (aspirational) feels excited — this aligns with her ideal self — and rates willingness to pay 7/10. Twin B (pragmatic) dismisses it as overpriced relative to her ₹100-200 norm and rates 2/10, despite identical price sensitivity profiles.

dimensions.dimension_name: Visual Packaging Semiotics and Shelf Appeal
dimensions.what_it_captures: How she reads and responds to specific visual design elements on packaging — color psychology, typography style, illustration vs photography, minimalism vs maximalism, cultural visual cues (Ayurvedic motifs vs modern clean design). Q42 only captures a broad preference for 'clinical-looking' packaging but doesn't reveal how she responds to specific visual hierarchies, color palettes, or design languages when making split-second shelf or scroll decisions.
dimensions.concept_test_impact: When shown two concept mockups — one with muted earth tones, serif fonts, and botanical illustrations vs one with bold colors, sans-serif fonts, and geometric patterns — her response would vary dramatically based on her visual semiotics processing. This affects perceived quality, brand trust, and concept-product fit.
dimensions.ad_test_impact: Ad creative using warm golden tones and handwritten fonts signals 'artisanal/natural' while cool blues and clean lines signal 'clinical/scientific.' Her visual processing preferences determine which creative direction builds credibility for sensitive-skin claims and which feels inauthentic, directly impacting ad engagement and brand perception.
dimensions.what_we_can_infer: She said 'clinical-looking' packaging (Q42) and wants 'simple/cute packaging' (Q49), but these are vague descriptors. We don't know if 'clinical' means pharmaceutical white or dermatologist-blue, if 'cute' means kawaii-style or minimalist-charming, or how she resolves the tension between clinical and cute. Her actual visual processing preferences remain unmeasured.
dimensions.example_variation: Both twins see a body wash concept with a pastel pink bottle featuring floral watercolor illustrations and cursive text. Twin A (who reads soft visuals as 'gentle/trustworthy for sensitive skin') rates packaging appeal 9/10 and perceives it as effective. Twin B (who reads the same visuals as 'frivolous/not serious about skin science') rates 3/10 and doubts the product's efficacy claims.

dimensions.dimension_name: Skepticism Toward Marketing Claims and Framing
dimensions.what_it_captures: Her baseline level of cynicism or trust when confronted with marketing language — whether she takes product claims at face value, discounts them partially, or actively counter-argues against them. The questions capture what claims attract her attention (Q50) and what sources she trusts (Q17), but not her psychological reactance to persuasion attempts or how she internally processes marketing framing when she recognizes she's being sold to.
dimensions.concept_test_impact: A concept claiming '72-hour freshness with Ayurvedic botanicals' would be taken at face value by a low-skepticism twin (boosting purchase intent) but trigger counter-arguing in a high-skepticism twin ('how can Ayurvedic ingredients guarantee 72 hours? That's a chemical claim dressed in natural language'). This fundamentally changes concept credibility scores.
dimensions.ad_test_impact: An influencer-style ad with enthusiastic product endorsement would feel authentic to a low-skepticism twin but trigger 'paid promotion' alarm bells in a high-skepticism twin, reducing trust and engagement. Similarly, before/after visuals (which she says she likes in Q57) could be perceived as genuine evidence or manipulated marketing depending on skepticism level.
dimensions.what_we_can_infer: She rates packaging claims 1/5 trust (Q17), was disappointed by a ₹350 purchase that 'underdelivered' (Q52-53), and a social media purchase was 'mediocre' (Q56) — suggesting some skepticism. But she also strongly believes in Ayurvedic claims (Q18) and selected '72Hr Freshness' as appealing (Q50). Whether she applies skepticism selectively or universally is unknown.
dimensions.example_variation: Both twins see a concept claiming 'dermatologist-tested Ayurvedic formula for sensitive skin — 95% saw reduced irritation.' Twin A (moderate skepticism) finds the combination of clinical evidence and Ayurvedic positioning credible and compelling — purchase intent 8/10. Twin B (high skepticism) thinks 'dermatologist-tested AND Ayurvedic is contradictory positioning — they're trying to have it both ways' — purchase intent 4/10.

dimensions.what_it_captures: How her physical context, emotional state, and situational factors at the moment of ad/concept exposure affect her receptivity — whether she's more open to new products when stressed vs relaxed, morning vs evening, alone vs with family, browsing leisurely vs task-oriented shopping. The questions capture that she's 'moody' (Q6) and 'mood-dependent' about grooming (Q32), but never measure how mood and context modulate her response to marketing stimuli.
dimensions.concept_test_impact: A concept shown during a relaxed evening browsing session might get thoughtful evaluation and higher appeal scores, while the same concept encountered during rushed weekday shopping might be dismissed. For someone self-described as moody, the variance in concept evaluation across mood states could be much larger than for a stable-mood person, making twin calibration critical.
dimensions.ad_test_impact: A calming, slow-paced ad might resonate deeply when she's in a stressed state (seeking comfort) but feel boring when she's energetic. Conversely, an upbeat, vibrant ad might irritate her in a low mood but engage her in a positive one. Without knowing her mood-receptivity patterns, twins would average out responses and miss the high variance that defines her actual behavior.
dimensions.what_we_can_infer: She explicitly identifies as 'moody' (Q6) and says grooming is 'mood-dependent' (Q32), confirming mood matters to her. But we don't know the DIRECTION of the effect — does a bad mood make her more susceptible to indulgent product messaging (comfort purchase) or more resistant to all marketing (defensive withdrawal)? The mechanism is completely unmeasured.
dimensions.example_variation: Both twins are shown a body wash ad featuring a woman unwinding after a long day with a luxurious shower ritual. Twin A (mood-congruent responder, currently stressed) feels deeply seen — 'this is exactly what I need' — and purchase intent spikes to 9/10. Twin B (mood-incongruent responder, currently stressed) feels annoyed by the idealized scenario — 'I don't have time for this' — and rates 3/10. Same profile, same ad, opposite reactions driven by how mood shapes receptivity.
